Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»WATCH: Epic late surge that saw Tatjana Smith win Olympic gold!

Watch a replay of the epic race, and incredible finish, that saw Tatjana Smith secure SA’s first Olympic gold medal in the 100m breaststroke.

Coming into the 100m Olympic breaststroke final on Monday night, Tatjana Smith was the clear favourite, but she touched and turned at the 50m mark trailing in fourth place.

However, a stunning late surge in the final 20m enabled her to touch the wall first in1.05.28toclaim South Africa’s first gold medalat theParis Games2024.

“I don’t think anyone can explain it,” she stated afterwards.

“Like I said last night, for me the emotions were really because it took a village to get me to where I am. I know they would have been proud even if I didn’t have a medal. But it was really for them; I swam for everyone that’s been there for me and believed with me.

“It was for our country as well, our hashtag for [Team SA’s campaign] is ‘For my Country’. And it really is, it’s just to inspire someone at home, for them to know they are not disqualified from the race and to know that they could potentially be an Olympic champion.”



Smith’s own Olympics tally has now grown to three, with two golds and a silver. One more will see her equal Chad le Clos on four, the most of any South African Olympian.

Her attention will next turn to defending her gold in the 200m.

It’s been an incredible journey over the last few years as Smith once admitted she was considering walking away from the pool.

“Sometimes when the expectations get too much you lose the fun and joy out of competing, and that definitely played a role in the last two years,” she said during an interview last year.

“It’s definitely been tough. You think there’s a lot of expectations but it’s mostly your own. You always think people will be disappointed if you don’t pull out the same performances as before. You just have to give your best; you can’t always be at your complete best.

“I don’t think any athlete has run, swam PBs left, right and centre every time they compete. You can never always have the perfect race and I think it was one of the challenges after the Olympics – it’s impossible for me not to expect a lot from myself. “
